findings.title: CA private key not deleted on restart failure (install_ca_cert returns None)
findings.description: In _restart_mitmproxy (src/main.py:394-403), when install_ca_cert() returns None, the function shuts down the new master and returns (None, None) — but never calls delete_ca_key_files(confdir) or delete_ca_public_cert(confdir). mitmproxy generates new CA key files in confdir during _start_mitmproxy. If certutil fails to install the cert, those key files remain on disk indefinitely. This violates the project's core security invariant: CA keys must only exist on disk during the brief startup window.
findings.impact: CA private key and public cert persist on disk in session_tmpdir until either (a) next successful restart or (b) application exit. If an attacker gains access to the session tmpdir during this window, they can use the CA key to issue arbitrary trusted certificates, enabling MITM attacks beyond the intended proxy scope.

findings.title: _last_flow_time written without lock despite spec requiring one
findings.description: proxy_addon.py:148 writes self._last_flow_time = time.monotonic() from the mitmproxy event loop without acquiring self._lock. The task spec explicitly states: 'use a lock to be safe' since this attribute is written from the mitmproxy event loop and read from the monitor thread. While CPython's GIL makes float assignment effectively atomic today, this is an implementation detail, not a language guarantee. Non-CPython runtimes (PyPy, GraalPy) or future CPython versions may not preserve this behavior.
findings.impact: Under a non-CPython runtime or with future changes, the monitor thread could read a torn float value, causing a spurious idle guard decision (allowing or blocking a restart based on corrupt data). Low probability but violates the spec's explicit requirement.
findings.recommendation:
Acquire self._lock when writing _last_flow_time:

def request(self, flow: http.HTTPFlow) -> None:
    with self._lock:
        self._last_flow_time = time.monotonic()  # track ALL traffic for idle guard
    if not is_target_domain(flow.request.host):
        return
    with self._lock:
        accept_lang = self._preset.accept_language
    flow.request.headers["Accept-Language"] = accept_lang

Alternatively use a dedicated threading.Lock for the timestamp if lock nesting is a concern.

findings.title: Stale CA cert may remain in system trust store if uninstall_ca_cert fails
findings.description: In _restart_mitmproxy (src/main.py:379-384), if uninstall_ca_cert() raises an exception it is only logged as a warning and execution continues. This means the old CA cert thumbprint (state.ca_thumbprint) remains installed in the Windows certificate store. After a successful restart, state.ca_thumbprint is updated to the new cert, so there is no path to clean up the orphaned old cert (neither the next restart nor program exit will reference the old thumbprint).
findings.impact: The old CA cert accumulates in the system trust store across restarts. While the old CA private key is already deleted from disk (so it cannot be used to issue new certificates), the stale cert enlarges the system trust surface and may be flagged by security compliance tools. After multiple restarts, multiple orphaned CA certs accumulate.

findings.title: sys.exit(1) in _start_mitmproxy bypasses exception handling in _restart_mitmproxy
findings.description: src/main.py:357-358: if mitmproxy fails to start within 15 seconds, _start_mitmproxy calls sys.exit(1). SystemExit is a subclass of BaseException, not Exception, so it is NOT caught by the 'except Exception as e' wrapper in _restart_mitmproxy (line 390) or by the outer RAM monitor 'except Exception as e' (line 628). If this happens during a restart, SystemExit propagates up through the daemon monitor thread, terminating it silently. The old master has already been shut down (step 1) so the proxy is dead, but the application continues running with no proxy and no monitor. The restart failure path (setting cooldown, returning None, None) is bypassed entirely.
findings.impact: If mitmproxy fails to start during restart, the application enters a degraded state: proxy is down, monitor thread is dead, no further restart attempts will occur. The user sees no error (SystemExit in a daemon thread exits the thread silently). Connectivity is lost for the session with no recovery path.
findings.recommendation:
Replace sys.exit(1) in _start_mitmproxy with a raised exception that _restart_mitmproxy can catch and handle:

# Instead of:
logger.error("mitmproxy failed to start within 15 seconds")
sys.exit(1)

# Use:
raise RuntimeError("mitmproxy failed to start within 15 seconds")

Note: sys.exit(1) in _start_mitmproxy was appropriate for the initial startup in main() (where exiting the process is correct behavior), but creates a problem when called from _restart_mitmproxy. Consider a parameter to control this behavior or extract the startup logic.
